EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked offshore of Southern New England Sunday into Monday bringing a long period of snow to the region, mainly eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island. Snow began after midnight on Sunday and tapered off from west to east early Monday morning. 6 to 9 inches fell centered over Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 7 to 9 inches. The highest amount reported was 9.6 inches in West Warwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving eastward from the eastern Great Lakes, triggered strong thunderstorms in New York state, which moved into southern New England overnight.  The atmosphere was very unstable and surface dewpoints were near 70 degrees. The result was strong to locally severe thunderstorms before daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Lincoln, a tree was down between Twin River Road and Breakneck Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough moving through northern New England combined with an advancing cold front to produce severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon and evening hours.  Despite low K Indices and dry air aloft, the storms managed to strengthen and the dry air helped enhance the severe wind gusts.  The storms mainly impacted areas from western MA east-southeastward into northern CT and northern RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Foster, police reported a tree down at the intersection of Route 94 (Foster Center Road) and Route 102. Report relayed via amateur radio operators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough moving through northern New England combined with an advancing cold front to produce severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon and evening hours.  Despite low K Indices and dry air aloft, the storms managed to strengthen and the dry air helped enhance the severe wind gusts.  The storms mainly impacted areas from western MA east-southeastward into northern CT and northern RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Glocester, a tree was down on Hunting House Road and a tree was down on Cranberry Ridge Road.  A little farther east, two large limbs were down on Snake Hill Road at Paris Irons Road.  Reports relayed via amateur radio operators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough moving through northern New England combined with an advancing cold front to produce severe thunderstorms in the late afternoon and evening hours.  Despite low K Indices and dry air aloft, the storms managed to strengthen and the dry air helped enhance the severe wind gusts.  The storms mainly impacted areas from western MA east-southeastward into northern CT and northern RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Greenville portion of Smithfield, multiple trees were down...on Maplecrest Drive, along Route 44, and on Arthur Street.  Reports relayed by amateur radio operators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the eastern seaboard Sunday night into Monday bringing heavy snow to western and central Massachusetts and snow changing to rain elsewhere. Strong winds caused some damage and there were some coastal flooding issues as well. Snow overspread the region between 10pm and 2 am before quickly changing to rain across most of CT, RI, and eastern MA. Strong to damaging winds impacted the south coast late Sunday night into Monday morning with moderate coastal flooding during the Mon morning high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cranston at 704 AM a tree was down on Cranston St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Explosive cyclogenesis of a low pressure center off the Mid Atlantic coast brought a strong winter storm with blizzard conditions to all of southern New England Saturday and Saturday night. The storm tracked from east of the Carolinas to the 70w/40n benchmark bringing extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour and winds gusting to hurricane force along the coast and 50 to 60 mph inland. The heaviest snow, just under 30 inches, fell over southeast MA, and in RI up to 2 feet fell. Snow tapered off overnight Saturday with quiet weather to follow on Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Providence Airport ASOS (KPVD) reported blizzard conditions from 921 AM EST to 158 PM EST and from 210 PM EST to 251 PM EST. Wind gusted 30 to 40 mph. Snowfall ranged from 12 to 19 inches. The highest snowfall report was 19.5 inches in Warwick.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Westerly Airport ASOS (KWST) met blizzard criteria from 911 AM EST to 1146 AM EST, from 1153 AM EST to 301 PM EST, and from 420 PM EST to 453 PM EST. The maximum gust from KWST was 44 mph at 1253 PM EST. Winds generally gusted 40 to 60 mph. The strongest gusts were 64 mph in New Shoreham at 929 PM EST and 64 mph at Ninigret Pond in Charlestown at 1038 PM EST. Snowfall ranged from 11 to 21 inches. The highest amount was 21 inches in Charlestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure passing southeast of New England brought widespread snow, especially for northern RI. Snow began around midnight and continued into Friday with the period of greatest snow accumulation in the early to late morning hours. This impacted travel during the morning commute. 10 to 14 inches fell in the I-95 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 8 to 12 inches with the highest amount (12 inches) reported in Harrisville and Burrillville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved northward from the mid-Atlantic coast late on the 18th to interior New England during the pre-dawn morning hours on the 19th.  Locally heavy rainfall helped bring down damaging wind gusts from a low-level jet across Rhode Island and southeast Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Narragansett at Point Judith, a Weatherflow mesonet station recorded a wind gust to 79 mph at 450 AM EDT (350 AM EST).  In Narragansett at 503 AM EDT (403 AM EST), the fire department reported a utility pole and wires down on Sand Hill Cove Road...and at 515 AM EDT (415 AM EST) police reported a large tree down on cable wires on Austin Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved northward from the mid-Atlantic coast late on the 18th to interior New England during the pre-dawn morning hours on the 19th.  Locally heavy rainfall helped bring down damaging wind gusts from a low-level jet across Rhode Island and southeast Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally were gusting to 55 to 75 mph in the heavier showers.  At Halfway Rock, just south of Prudence Island, a Weatherflow mesonet site measured a gust to 68 mph at 449 AM EDT (349 AM EST).  In Jamestown at 448 AM EDT (348 AM EST), fire department officials reported power lines down on North Main Road.  In Little Compton at 557 AM EDT (457 AM EST), police reported two oak trees down on wires (street not given).  In Newport at 611 AM EDT (511 AM EST),  the fire department reported a tree down on Harrison Avenue at Ridge Road.  All of the aforementioned reports were relayed by amateur radio operators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and unseasonably mild weather with near record temperatures preceded a period of strong winds late Thursday into Friday. A shortwave trough moving toward the Great Lakes combined with a surface cold front moving through late Thursday brought these winds along with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally gusted 40 to 55 mph. The TF Green Airport ASOS (KPVD) gusted to 62 mph at 604 AM EST. In East Greenwich at 11:09 AM EST there was a small tree down on Partridge Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and unseasonably mild weather with near record temperatures preceded a period of strong winds late Thursday into Friday. A shortwave trough moving toward the Great Lakes combined with a surface cold front moving through late Thursday brought these winds along with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally gusted 40 to 60 mph. At Fogland Beach in Tiverton winds gusted to 66 mph at 5:13 AM EST. On Rose Island winds gusted to 54 mph at 7:59 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and unseasonably mild weather with near record temperatures preceded a period of strong winds late Thursday into Friday. A shortwave trough moving toward the Great Lakes combined with a surface cold front moving through late Thursday brought these winds along with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the Block Island Jetty winds gusted up to 65 mph at 7:17 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and unseasonably mild weather with near record temperatures preceded a period of strong winds late Thursday into Friday. A shortwave trough moving toward the Great Lakes combined with a surface cold front moving through late Thursday brought these winds along with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cranston at 7:30 AM EST a large tree was blocking Crocus Dr, there was a tree down on Fletcher Ave at Britton St, and there were power lines down on Julia St at Legion Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and unseasonably mild weather with near record temperatures preceded a period of strong winds late Thursday into Friday. A shortwave trough moving toward the Great Lakes combined with a surface cold front moving through late Thursday brought these winds along with heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Barrington at 6:20 AM EST a tree was down blocking the road on Middle Hwy and South Lake Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Canadian Maritimes, producing strong westerly winds across southern New England. Gusts mainly were in the 45 to 55 mph range, but reached 63 mph on Block Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 827 PM EST on March 12, a Weatherflow station at the Block Island Jetty recorded a wind gust to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough produced a band of showers and thunderstorms, with locally torrential rainfall, that moved quickly through during the pre-dawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cranston at 430 AM EDT (330 AM EST), a tree and wires were brought down onto a home due to a lightning strike.  Street not specified.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through during the mid-afternoon, triggering isolated severe thunderstorms in eastern Connecticut and Rhode Island.  The presence of dry air aloft helped keep the storm coverage isolated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In West Greenwich, two trees were down on Robin Hollow Road. Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front across Rhode Island and southeast Massachusetts provided the focus for heavy rain and scattered thunderstorms.  Four to six inches of rain fell in the greater Providence area, with a max of 7.44 inches in Rehoboth, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In East Providence, at 310 PM EDT (210 EST) flash flooding caused the blockage of all travel lanes on Interstate 195 westbound at Exit 2.  At 323 PM EDT (225 PM EST), a car was reportedly stuck in flood waters on Roma Street.  At 402 PM EDT (302 PM EST), Broadway was completely flooded between Juniper and Laura Streets.  Also in East Providence, at 520 PM EDT (420 PM EST), Waterman Avenue was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front across Rhode Island and southeast Massachusetts provided the focus for heavy rain and scattered thunderstorms.  Four to six inches of rain fell in the greater Providence area, with a max of 7.44 inches in Rehoboth, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cumberland, Abbott Street was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front across Rhode Island and southeast Massachusetts provided the focus for heavy rain and scattered thunderstorms.  Four to six inches of rain fell in the greater Providence area, with a max of 7.44 inches in Rehoboth, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Central Falls, a car was stuck in flash flooding on Higginson Avenue.  Water was reportedly up to some peoples' knees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a warm and humid air mass, triggered scattered severe thunderstorms in southern New England during the mid and late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Hope Valley section of Hopkinton, amateur radio reported that a large tree fell on top of a shed, destroying the shed.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a hot and steamy environment to create strong to severe thunderstorms across southeastern MA and eastern RI during the late afternoon and evening.  There was some temporary, but significant, urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Bristol, a house fire was started by lightning on Metacom Avenue.  Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a warm and humid air mass, triggered scattered severe thunderstorms in southern New England during the mid and late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Richmond, a tree was down on Gardiner Road and additional trees and wires were down off of Gardiner Road and Hoxsie Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across southern New England, causing southerly winds that gusted to 40 to 55 mph.  Although leaves were gone from many trees, there was still some scattered tree damage in northern Rhode Island, northern Connecticut, and in Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Chepachet section of Glocester, a tree was down in the 1600 block of Snake Hill Road.  Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across southern New England, causing southerly winds that gusted to 40 to 55 mph.  Although leaves were gone from many trees, there was still some scattered tree damage in northern Rhode Island, northern Connecticut, and in Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Scituate, a large tree was down at the intersection of Plainfield Pike and Wilbur Hollow Road. Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through southern New England during the morning bringing flooding to portions of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In New Shoreham, 6 to 12 inches of street flooding was reported on Corn Neck Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through southern New England during the morning bringing flooding to portions of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Narragansett, the emergency room entrance of South County Hospital was flooded. Boston Neck Rd. (Rt 1A) near Narragansett Ave was closed due to flooding. Several inches of street flooding was reported along the Rt. 1 onramp past the rotary. Basements were reported flooded on Brown St. (2 feet of flooding) and on Kingstown Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence, 7 to 10 inches of rain fell over the course of a few hours during the mid to late afternoon creating serious flash flooding. 8.31 inches of rain fell in the Elmwood section of the city and 6.89 in the College Hill section.  National news media showed aerial views of Interstate 95 which had become completely flooded and was closed in both directions. The snarled traffic lasted for multiple hours. Cars were reported to be stuck in the following locations: Webster Ave., Hamilton St., Cadillac Ave. at Melrose St. (water up to the windows), Narragansett Ave at Santiago St., Rugby St. at Thurbers Ave. Additional flooding and road closure reports included: Thackery St. at Melrose St., the underpass of Rt. 10 north at Westminster St., Hartford Ave. at Heath St., Smith St. at Gentian Ave., Exchange St., Pleasant Valley Parkway at Berlin St., Huntington Ave., Manton Ave. at Saban St., Elmwood Ave. ramp to Rt. 10, and Olneyville Square.  One block of Atwells Avenue was washed out. Many water rescues were necessary due to life-threatening situations.  All together, there were 3 injuries related to motor vehicle accidents during the storm and roughly 25 individuals rescued.  On Benevolent St. water invaded the 1st floor of a Brown University campus building. On Elmwood Ave. there was rapid flooding of a structure of unspecified type.  On Peace Street, there was a partial (25%) collapse of the roof of a vacant building, which was being redeveloped into a shopping plaza.  A total of 41 homes and businesses were flooded.  The West River overtopped its banks and flooded Charles Street and portions of Silver Spring Street in the evening.  The Woonasquatucket River crested at 6.07 ft. at 315 AM on September 7th, which was above the 5.5 ft. flood stage.  Many thanks to the Providence Emergency Management Agency for some of these details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In North Providence, Library Rd. by Adams Library was flooded and impassable. Relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence County, very heavy rain fell, eventually amounting to 7 to 10 inches. Approximately 6 inches fell in 2 hours leading to flash flooding. At 1:51 PM EDT Oregon St. at Whipple St. was closed.  At 2:05 PM EDT a car was stuck in street flooding on Valley St. at Eagle St. Cars were traveling in the wrong direction to avoid flooding on Pleasant Valley Parkway. At 2:26 PM EDT Dexter St. at Huntington Ave. was reported to be flooded and impassable. Relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cranston, at 2:02 PM EDT a car was stuck in street flooding on Fletcher Ave. Between 2:04 and 2:11 PM EDT multiple cars were stuck in street flooding on Atwood Ave. At 2:13 PM EDT Rt. 10 north at Union Ave was flooded and impassable with cars stuck in the flooding. Relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cranston, very heavy rain fell totaling 8 to 11 inches. More than 6 inches fell in 2 hours during the mid afternoon. Five cars were stuck in flash flooding on Cranston St. Three cars were stuck on Oaklawn Ave. Rt. 10 was both northbound and southbound was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Smithfield, the public reported a 7.95 inch rainfall total and a CoCoRaHS observer in Greenville reported 7.80 inches. The AWOS station at North Central State Airport (KSFZ) reported 6.13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened rapidly as it tracked through the eastern Great Lakes.|The storm produced damaging southeast to south winds across much of southern New England and minor to moderate coastal flooding the east and south coasts.  Two to 3.5 inches of rain fell across much of RI and interior MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Newport, at 633 AM, police reported that the Sachuest Point Refuge and Sachuest Point Road were flooded over by coastal flooding and, later, King Park was flooded. In Jamestown at 730 AM, a trained spotter reported water and debris in an unspecified roadway.  At 815 AM, amateur radio operators reported that a main road on Prudence Island was flooded, which isolated the northern and southern parts of the island.  At 920 AM, also on Prudence Island, there was 6 to 12 inches of street flooding and significant dock flooding on Neck Farm Road.  In Middletown, Lower Aquidneck Avenue was flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Charlestown at 242 PM, the Weatherflow mesonet station at Ninigret Pond measured a wind gust to 65 mph.  Also in Charlestown, at 515 PM, a CWOP station recorded a wind gust to 61 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The total water level at the Fox Point Barrier reached 9.37 feet - which was a 3 to 4 foot surge above astronomical tide.  This caused the barrier to be shut down (6.7 feet is their threshold).  Other area streets in Providence had several inches of coastal flooding.

In Warren at 830 AM, there was a social media post of coastal flooding on Main Street.  In Barrington, the broadcast media Tweeted a photo of a completely flooded Matthewson Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence at 1249 PM, a large tree was down on Wolcott Street near Regent Avenue. Winds generally were gusting to 55 to 60 mph.  A gust to 64 mph was recorded a little farther south at T.F. Green Airport in Warwick, RI (KPVD) at 310 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified along the New Jersey coast on the 16th and moved northeast across southeast New England, reaching the Gulf of Maine on the 17th.  The big impact was very heavy elevational snows in MA. Elsewhere there was a few inches of rain and gusty winds, both ahead of and behind the system. In RI, there was an isolated report of strong winds early on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Tiverton, police reported a tree down on Lake Road.  Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over southern New England causing very heavy rain and significant flash flooding before the front moved off the south coast during the evening. Rainfall amounts ranged from 7 to 11 inches in eastern Connecticut and the Providence metro area.
